text: Hello and welcome to this video article presenting the Edinburgh Posterior Approach to Vax Pneumonectomy. What we're looking at here is the CT scan of a seven-year-old lady presented with an incidental finding of a left upper lobe lesion on chest x-ray. PET CT revealed this fifty by thirty millimetre T-two-B-N-naught-M-naught lesion which as you can see from the scan is very close to the aorta and pulmonary artery. The further concern was that the tumour was invading at the level of the orifice of the left upper lobe.

Based on the imaging available and observations at the time of surgery, it was decided to attempt a pneumonectomy as opposed to a bronchovascular sleeve where the risk of a positive resection margin would be high.

The surgeon dissects into the sub-amphetitial plane to remove diseased mediastinal pleura. The superior intercostal vein is clipped and divided. The vagus nerve is divided due to necessity. A blunt instrument is used to reflect the pleura, examining the posterior hilum for involvement of the left bronchus, pulmonary artery, and recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon uses diathermy shears to remove the pleura overlying the bronchus, aiming to skeletonize the bronchus through sharp and blunt dissection. Station five lymph nodes are visible to the right of the bronchus, while station seven lymph nodes appear to the left. The station seven node is sent for frozen section analysis, confirming no metastatic involvement, which aids in clearing the subcarinal space.

The surgeon passes a right-angled Roberts clamp anterior to the bronchus, carefully avoiding the pulmonary artery. A vascular sling is placed in the clamp's jaws for retraction, enhancing the view of the proximal bronchus and facilitating the removal of station five lymph nodes with node holding forceps. The focus shifts to the inferior and anterior hilum, where blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to dissect the inferior pulmonary ligament. Anteriorly, the pleura is dissected posterior to the phrenic nerve, allowing assessment of the pulmonary vein, which appears free from disease.

The surgeon passes the Roberts behind the superior pulmonary vein, which is then transected using a TAN-45 tri-stapler through the posterior port. The bronchus is retracted with a vascular sling, allowing it to be taken close to the carina with a stapler passed through the anterior port, while ensuring the pulmonary artery lying anterior to the bronchus is not caught.

After dividing the bronchus, the surgeon gains clear views of the pulmonary artery. The Roberts clamp is passed behind the artery to create a passageway for the Tan-Forty-Five Tri-Stapler. The stapler is inserted through the anterior utility port and positioned flush against the pulmonary artery, with gentle rotation to ensure proper alignment before firing.

The surgeon maneuvers the Tan-Forty-Five tri-stapler into position around the inferior pulmonary vein, carefully aligning it on either side of the vein. The stapler is then closed and fired to complete the division of the vein, preventing vascular engorgement.

The surgeon completes the visualization of the hilar structures, achieving haemostasis. The specimen is removed through a plastic bag to prevent contamination and seeding in the wound. The bronchus is checked for air leaks, and a paravertebral catheter is placed adjacent to the sympathetic chain. A chest drain is also inserted.

The video shows an eight-centimeter utility port and its orientation relative to other incisions. A post-operative day one chest X-ray is displayed, indicating the satisfactory position of the mediastinum.
This video was produced in the Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh in the Cardiothoracic Unit and we thank you for your viewing of our production.
